meaning: Sexual violation of the listener's mother.
cultural_context: The nuclear option of Chinese profanity. The character 肏 is composed of 入 (enter) over 肉 (meat), visually depicting penetration. Often censored, replaced with homophones 操 (cāo - exercise) or 草 (cǎo - grass). The 'Grass Mud Horse' (草泥马 - cǎo ní mǎ) meme uses these exact tones to say 'fuck your mother' while appearing to discuss an alpaca.
example_usage: 肏你妈！ (Cào nǐ mā! - Fuck your mother!)

meaning: Homophonic substitution for 肏你妈 (fuck your mother).
cultural_context: The most famous example of Chinese internet censorship evasion. 草 (cǎo - grass) sounds like 肏 (cào - fuck), 泥 (ní - mud) sounds like 你 (nǐ - you), 马 (mǎ - horse) sounds like 妈 (mā - mother). The 'Grass Mud Horse' became a viral meme and actual alpaca mascot for free speech. Demonstrates how Chinese netizens use tonal homophones to bypass the Great Firewall.
example_usage: 草泥马！真麻烦！ (Cǎo ní mǎ! What a hassle! - appears innocent, means FYM)

meaning: Possessive phrase implying 'fuck his mother'; used as universal exclamation.
cultural_context: The most common Chinese exclamation, used daily by millions. Abbreviated as TMD in texting. Comparable to English 'fuck' or 'damn' in versatility. '你他妈的' (nǐ tā mā de) = 'you fucking...' (your mother's). Lu Xun wrote a famous essay arguing this phrase is China's 'national curse.'
example_usage: 他妈的！下雨了！ (Tā mā de! It's raining! - Damn it! It's raining!)

meaning: Stupid person; literally 'stupid vagina.'
cultural_context: Extremely common, abbreviated as SB in internet slang. The character 屄 (bī - cunt) is often replaced with 逼 (bī - force) or B to evade filters. '2B' (two-B) is variant using number for shape similarity. Can be playful among friends but highly offensive to strangers. Yelled by thousands at football matches.
example_usage: 你这个傻逼！ (Nǐ zhège shǎbī! - You stupid cunt!)

meaning: Romanized abbreviation for 傻逼.
cultural_context: Texting/online abbreviation to evade character filters. '2B' replaces S with 2 due to visual similarity. Common in gaming and social media. Milder visual impact than characters but same meaning.
example_usage: 你真SB！ (Nǐ zhēn SB! - You're so SB/dumb!)

meaning: Awesome, cool, powerful; literally 'cow vagina.'
cultural_context: PARADOXICAL USAGE: Despite anatomical reference, this is POSITIVE. Abbreviated as NB. 'Niú' (cow/bull) implies strength; 'bī' intensifies. Calling someone NB is high praise. 'Zhuangbi' (装逼 - pretend-badass) means poser/fake. Demonstrates how Chinese profanity context determines meaning completely.
example_usage: 太牛屄了！ (Tài niúbī le! - So fucking awesome!)

meaning: Abbreviation for 牛屄 (awesome).
cultural_context: Common internet slang for 'cool' or 'impressive.' Used positively unlike SB. Can be ironic depending on context.
example_usage: NB！ (NB! - Awesome!)

word: 肏你祖宗十八代 (cào nǐ zǔzōng shíbā dài)
english_equivalent: fuck your whole lineage / damn your bloodline
meaning: Curse targeting the listener's entire ancestry back 18 generations.
cultural_context: THE ULTIMATE CHINESE INSULT. In Confucian culture where ancestor worship is central, this attacks the entire family line. 18 generations covers approximately 450 years of ancestry. Used only when intending to make permanent enemies. Guarantees escalation to physical violence.
example_usage: 肏你祖宗十八代！ (Cào nǐ zǔzōng shíbā dài! - Fuck your ancestors 18 generations!)
ipa_pronunciation: tsʰau ni tsu tsʊŋ ʂʰʅ pa tai

meaning: Person of low moral character; cheap in value.
cultural_context: Strong insult implying the person has no self-respect or dignity. 'Jiàn' (贱) = cheap/low. 'Jiàn huò' (贱货 - cheap goods) is variant, more severe. Often gendered but can apply to men.
example_usage: 你这个贱人！ (Nǐ zhège jiànrén! - You bitch/lowlife!)

meaning: Dehumanizing term comparing person to worthless merchandise.
cultural_context: Objectifies the person as commodity with no value. Stronger than 贱人. Gendered female in usage though grammatically neutral.
example_usage: 贱货！ (Jiàn huò! - You cheap trash!)

meaning: Woman who sells sex; promiscuous woman.
cultural_context: Classical Chinese insult appearing in literature for centuries. 'Biao' (婊) specifically denotes prostitution. Extremely offensive to women. Can end relationships immediately if used to girlfriend/wife.
example_usage: 婊子养的！ (Biǎozi yǎng de! - Son of a whore! / Raised by a whore!)

meaning: Person of mixed/unpure origins; bastard.
cultural_context: Egg-based insult implying uncertain parentage. 'Hun' (混) = mixed/turbid. Less severe than 王八蛋 (turtle egg). Common in films and daily arguments.
example_usage: 你这个混蛋！ (Nǐ zhège húndàn! - You bastard!)

english_equivalent: son of a bitch / bastard / turtle egg
meaning: Bastard; literally 'turtle egg.'
cultural_context: Turtles/tortoises symbolize cuckoldry in Chinese culture (wearing 'green hat'). 'Wangba' implies the mother cheated. 'Wang ba' (忘八) also sounds like 'forget eight' (referring to 8 Confucian virtues). Common insult, less severe than sexual terms.
example_usage: 王八蛋！敢骗我！ (Wángbādàn! How dare you lie to me!)

meaning: Fool; literally 'stupid egg.'
cultural_context: Mild insult suitable for children and friends. 'Ben' (笨) = stupid/clumsy. Egg metaphors in Chinese insults imply emptiness or fragility.
example_usage: 笨蛋！钥匙在这儿！ (Bèndàn! The key is here! - Silly! The key is here!)

meaning: Morally bad person.
cultural_context: Can be affectionate ('小坏蛋' - little rascal) or serious. Common in parental scolding of children.
example_usage: 你这个坏蛋！ (Nǐ zhège huàidàn! - You bad egg!)

english_equivalent: fuck off / get lost / beat it
meaning: Roll away; command to leave.
cultural_context: Brusque dismissal. 'Gǔn kāi' (滚开 - roll away) is stronger. 'Gǔn dàn' (滚蛋 - roll egg) means 'scram.' Less vulgar than sexual terms but very rude due to directness.
example_usage: 滚！别烦我！ (Gǔn! Bié fán wǒ! - Fuck off! Don't bother me!)

english_equivalent: go to hell / fuck off / get lost
meaning: Milder way to tell someone to go away.
cultural_context: Euphemistic, avoids direct sexual reference. Comparable to 'go away' or 'oh, go on.' Safe for casual use.
example_usage: 去你的！别吹牛了！ (Qù nǐ de! Stop bragging! - Oh, go on! Stop bragging!)

meaning: Person who flatters superiors; brown-noser.
cultural_context: Idiomatic insult based on equestrian imagery. 'Pai mapi' implies risk of getting kicked by the horse, showing foolishness of the flatterer. Common workplace insult.
example_usage: 他总是拍马屁 (Tā zǒngshì pāi mǎpì - He always brown-noses)

meaning: Abnormal person; pervert; psychologically deviant.
cultural_context: Used for both actual perverts and people acting strangely. Also used for extremely spicy food ('变态辣' - pervertedly hot). Context determines offensiveness.
example_usage: 你这个变态！ (Nǐ zhège biàntài! - You pervert!)

meaning: Mentally ill person; crazy person.
cultural_context: Medical term repurposed as insult. Less clinical than English 'psychopath.' Can be playful ('你有神经病啊?' - Are you crazy?) or serious.
example_usage: 你神经病啊！ (Nǐ shénjīngbìng a! - Are you psycho!)

meaning: The number 250; slang for idiot.
cultural_context: Origin story: Ancient king offered 1000 taels for his minister's killer. Four men claimed credit, dividing reward into 250 each, revealing their stupidity. 'Er' (二 - two) alone also means stupid. Avoid pricing things at 250 RMB as it offends customers.
example_usage: 你这个二百五！ (Nǐ zhège èrbǎiwǔ! - You idiot!)

meaning: Loser; failure; socially undesirable male.
cultural_context: Originally vulgar insult (dick hair), reclaimed by 530 million Chinese as self-deprecating identity. Counterpart to 'gaofushuai' (tall-rich-handsome). Represents ordinary struggles vs elite success. Xiaomi founder Lei Jun embraced it. Now mainstream identifier for 'ordinary person.'
example_usage: 我就是个屌丝 (Wǒ jiùshì ge diǎosī - I'm just a loser/ordinary guy)

meaning: Unsophisticated person from rural areas; bumpkin.
cultural_context: Implies lack of exposure to modern urban culture. 'Tu' (土) = earth/uncultured. 'Bie' (鳖) = soft-shelled turtle. Contrast to '海龟' (haigui - sea turtle) for foreign-educated returnees.
example_usage: 你真是个土鳖 (Nǐ zhēnshì ge tǔbiē - You're such a bumpkin)

english_equivalent: fake sweet girl / gold digger / seeming innocent but scheming
meaning: Woman who appears innocent/pure (like green tea) but is actually manipulative/promiscuous.
cultural_context: Modern internet slang for women who pretend to be innocent while scheming to seduce men. 'Green tea' symbolizes purity in Chinese culture; the insult exposes the hypocrisy.
example_usage: 她是个绿茶婊 (Tā shì ge lǜchábiǎo - She's a green tea bitch/fake innocent)

meaning: Third person in relationship; mistress.
cultural_context: Numerical slang: third wheel in marriage (wife = 1, husband = 2, mistress = 3). Socially stigmatized role in Chinese culture where extramarital affairs are common but condemned.
example_usage: 他是小三 (Tā shì xiǎosān - She is the mistress)

meaning: To take sexual advantage; to grope; to harass.
cultural_context: Euphemism for sexual harassment (referencing tofu's soft/white texture like skin). 'Don't eat my tofu' = don't sexually harass me. Innocuous sounding but describes serious misconduct.
example_usage: 别吃我豆腐 (Bié chī wǒ dòufu - Don't eat my tofu - Don't harass me)

meaning: To sell one's body; prostitution.
cultural_context: Euphemism for prostitution. Origin: soft texture of tofu implies female body. Be careful when asking if someone likes tofu—it may be misinterpreted.
example_usage: 她在卖豆腐 (Tā zài mài dòufu - She's selling tofu - She's a prostitute)

english_equivalent: poser / show-off / fake it till you make it
meaning: To pretend to be awesome; to pose; to show off.
cultural_context: Combination of 装 (zhuāng - pretend/act) and 逼 (bī - badass/cunt). Accuses someone of pretending to be NB (牛屄 - awesome) when they're not. Major social sin in Chinese culture valuing authenticity.
example_usage: 别装逼了！ (Bié zhuāngbī le! - Stop pretending/posing!)

english_equivalent: son of a bitch / dog thing / cur
meaning: Contemptible person; literally 'dog thing/object.'
cultural_context: Dogs have negative connotations in traditional Chinese culture (unlike Western positive associations). 'Dog thing' implies the person is not even human, just an object like a dog.
example_usage: 这个狗东西！ (Zhège gǒudōngxi! - This son of a bitch!)

meaning: Bastard; literally 'dog-fucked' or born of dog intercourse.
cultural_context: Implies the person's mother had sex with dogs. Extremely vulgar. Regional variant uses different characters but same meaning. Highly offensive in any context.
example_usage: 狗日的！敢骗我！ (Gǒurìde! How dare you lie to me!)

meaning: Turtle; slang for cuckold or bastard.
cultural_context: Turtles symbolize cuckoldry (wearing green hat). Calling someone 'wangba' implies their partner cheats on them or they are illegitimate. Short for 王八蛋 (turtle egg).
example_usage: 你这个王八！ (Nǐ zhège wángba! - You bastard!)

word: 戴绿帽子 (dài lǜ mào zi)
meaning: To be cuckolded; to have an unfaithful wife.
cultural_context: Historical origin: Yuan Dynasty decree required families of prostitutes to wear green head coverings. Now symbolizes infidelity. Giving someone a green hat is fighting words. Green hats avoided as gifts.
example_usage: 他戴绿帽子了 (Tā dài lǜmàozi le - He's been cuckolded)

meaning: Chicken; slang for prostitute.
cultural_context: Homophone with 妓 (jì - prostitute). 'Xiao jie' (小姐 - Miss) became euphemism for prostitute in Mainland China, so 'chicken' (鸡) is used instead. 'Zuo ji' (坐鸡) = working as prostitute.
example_usage: 她是一只鸡 (Tā shì yī zhī jī - She is a chicken - She is a prostitute)

meaning: Duck; slang for male prostitute.
cultural_context: Male counterpart to 'chicken' (prostitute). 'Ya' (鸭) = duck. Male sex workers in China are called 'ducks' while females are 'chickens.'
example_usage: 他做鸭子 (Tā zuò yāzi - He works as a gigolo)

meaning: Abbreviation for 他妈的 (tā mā de - damn/fuck).
cultural_context: Common texting abbreviation. Bypasses character filters. 'TMD' looks like English acronym but represents Chinese profanity. Used frequently in online gaming and social media.
example_usage: TMD！又输了！ (TMD! Lost again! - Damn! Lost again!)

meaning: Abbreviation for 变态 (biàntài - pervert).
cultural_context: Texting slang. Can describe actual perversion or just silly/unusual behavior depending on context. 'Hen BT' = very perverted/very crazy.
example_usage: 你真BT！ (Nǐ zhēn BT! - You're so perverted/crazy!)

word: 你疯了吗 (nǐ fēng le ma)
english_equivalent: are you crazy? / are you insane?
meaning: Are you crazy? Questioning sanity.
cultural_context: Common expression of disbelief. Not strictly profanity but confrontational. Used when someone does something irrational or dangerous.
example_usage: 你疯了吗？开这么快！ (Nǐ fēngle ma? Driving so fast! - Are you crazy?)

word: 脑子进水 (nǎo zi jìn shuǐ)
english_equivalent: brain flooded / water on the brain / idiot
meaning: Stupid person; literally 'water entered brain.'
cultural_context: Implies malfunctioning brain. Medical metaphor for stupidity. 'Ni nao zi jin shui le?' = Are you stupid?/Did water get in your brain?
example_usage: 你脑子进水了？ (Nǐ nǎozi jìnshuǐ le? - Did water get in your brain? - Are you stupid?)

meaning: Crazy; mentally ill.
cultural_context: Accusing someone of being mentally ill. Can be playful among friends or serious insult. 'You bing a?' = Are you sick/crazy?
example_usage: 你有病啊？ (Nǐ yǒubìng a? - Are you sick/crazy?)

meaning: Mild insult calling someone stupid. Often used jokingly among friends or toward children.
cultural_context: Very mild, almost affectionate when used between close friends or family. The 'egg' suffix softens the insult.
example_usage: 你这个笨蛋！ (Nǐ zhège bèndàn!) - You idiot! (playful)

meaning: Playful term for someone acting foolish. Can be affectionate between couples or close friends.
cultural_context: Often used as a term of endearment rather than a true insult. Comparable to English 'silly goose'.
example_usage: 你真傻！ (Nǐ zhēn shǎ!) - You're so silly!

meaning: Implies someone is acting irrationally or crazy. Originally a medical term for neurological disorders.
cultural_context: Casual among friends but can offend if tone is harsh. Mental health awareness is making this term more controversial.
example_usage: 你神经病啊？ (Nǐ shénjīngbìng a?) - Are you crazy?

meaning: Someone who is petty, stingy, or unwilling to share/spend money.
cultural_context: Light teasing about someone's frugality. The 'ghost' (鬼) suffix adds playful tone.
example_usage: 别做小气鬼！ (Bié zuò xiǎoqìguǐ!) - Don't be such a cheapskate!

meaning: Someone who meddles in others' business or spreads gossip. Refers to the eight trigrams in Taoist cosmology.
cultural_context: Common in Hong Kong and Taiwan pop culture. Not strongly offensive but implies lack of discretion.
example_usage: 你很八卦！ (Nǐ hěn bāguà!) - You're so nosy!

meaning: Someone from the countryside perceived as unsophisticated or uncultured.
cultural_context: Classist insult reflecting urban-rural divide in China. Implies lack of exposure to modern/city culture.
example_usage: 你真是个土包子。 (Nǐ zhēnshi ge tǔbāozi.) - You're such a bumpkin.

meaning: Highly vulgar term combining stupidity with female genitalia. Extremely offensive.
cultural_context: One of the most common vulgar insults among younger generations. Highly offensive and should be avoided in all polite company.
example_usage: 你这个傻逼！ (Nǐ zhège shǎbī!) - You stupid cunt!

meaning: Command to leave immediately, literally 'roll away'.
cultural_context: Extremely dismissive and aggressive. Used in heated arguments or to show complete contempt.
example_usage: 滚出去！(Gǔn chūqù!) - Get out! / Fuck off!

meaning: Wish for someone's death. Extremely offensive and aggressive.
cultural_context: In Chinese culture where ancestor worship and longevity are valued, wishing death is particularly taboo and hostile.
example_usage: 你去死吧！(Nǐ qù sǐ ba!) - Go to hell!

meaning: Ancient insult implying illegitimacy. 'Wangba' refers to a turtle (symbol of cuckoldry), implying one's mother was unfaithful.
cultural_context: Classic insult implying the person's mother cheated (turtles don't know their fathers). Attacks family honor severely.
example_usage: 这个王八蛋骗了我！(Zhège wángbādàn piàn le wǒ!) - That bastard cheated me!

meaning: Someone of low moral character, cheap, or despicable. Often gendered toward women.
cultural_context: Implies someone lacks self-respect or dignity. Particularly offensive when directed at women, implying promiscuity or low virtue.
example_usage: 你这个贱人！(Nǐ zhège jiànrén!) - You bitch!

meaning: Dehumanizing term comparing person to worthless goods. Implies complete lack of intelligence.
cultural_context: Reducing someone to 'goods' denies their humanity. Serious insult to intelligence.
example_usage: 别做蠢货。 (Bié zuò chǔnhuò.) - Don't be an idiot.

meaning: Someone morally rotten, corrupt, or of very low character.
cultural_context: Implies fundamental moral corruption. Less vulgar but deeply insulting to character.
example_usage: 他是个烂人。(Tā shì ge lànrén.) - He's a scumbag.

meaning: Harsh term calling someone a ghost. Can be affectionate between couples but insulting in arguments.
cultural_context: Between spouses can be playful ('you devil'). In arguments, implies the person is already dead to the speaker.
example_usage: 你这个死鬼！(Nǐ zhège sǐguǐ!) - You damn devil!

meaning: The most vulgar verb meaning 'to fuck.' Often written with substitute character 操 (exercise) to avoid censorship.
cultural_context: Ultimate profanity in Mandarin. The original character 肏 combines 'enter' (入) and 'meat' (肉). Using 操 (exercise) is a euphemism.
example_usage: 操！(Cào!) - Fuck!

meaning: The most common Chinese expletive. Literally 'his mother's' but used as all-purpose profanity.
cultural_context: Ubiquitous across all social classes. Shortened to 妈的 (mā de). Attacks family indirectly but considered vulgar.
example_usage: 他妈的！我忘了！(Tā mā de! Wǒ wàng le!) - Damn! I forgot!

meaning: The gravest insult in Mandarin. Directly attacks the target's mother sexually.
cultural_context: EXTREME: Attacking someone's mother is the worst offense in Chinese culture due to Confucian filial piety. Can provoke physical violence.
example_usage: 操你妈！(Cào nǐ mā!) - Motherfucker!

english_equivalent: are you crazy? / are you stupid?
meaning: Rhetorical question implying someone is thinking irrationally or stupidly.
cultural_context: Indirect insult using metaphor. Softer than calling someone stupid directly but still offensive.
example_usage: 你脑子进水了吧？会相信这个！(Nǐ nǎozi jìn shuǐ le ba? Huì xiāngxìn zhège!) - Are you crazy? Believing this!
ipa_pronunciation: [nǐ nâu ts] ̌ tɕîn ʂwěi lə pa]

literal_translation: dog eyes look down on people
meaning: Accuses someone of being arrogant and looking down on others. Dogs symbolize contemptible behavior.
cultural_context: Classic idiom implying the person has 'dog eyes' that can't see human dignity. Attacks character through animal metaphor.
example_usage: 别狗眼看人低！(Bié gǒuyǎnkànréndī!) - Don't look down on me!

literal_translation: too full and have nothing better to do
meaning: Mocking someone for meddling in affairs that don't concern them. Implies they have excess energy.
cultural_context: Indirect criticism wrapped in concern about digestion. Common way to tell someone to mind their own business.
example_usage: 你是吃饱了撑的吧？(Nǐ shì chī bǎo le chēng de ba?) - You've got nothing better to do?

literal_translation: dog's mouth can't spit out ivory
meaning: Implies everything the person says is foul or base. Can't expect cultured words from an uncultured person.
cultural_context: Idiomatic expression suggesting the person's words are as worthless as a dog's bark. Attacks verbal expression.
example_usage: 狗嘴里吐不出象牙。(Gǒuzuǐ lǐ tǔ bù chū xiàngyá.) - Nothing good comes from your mouth.
ipa_pronunciation: [kǒu tswěi lǐ tʰǔ pu ʈʂʰʰû ɕjâŋ jâ]

meaning: Someone who flatters authority figures excessively. From the phrase 'patting horse's rear' (拍马屁).
cultural_context: Common workplace insult. Implies the person produces nothing but 'farts' of flattery.
example_usage: 他是个马屁精。(Tā shì ge mǎpìjīng.) - He's an ass-kisser.

meaning: Slang for stupid person. Origin: ancient monetary unit or reference to historical figures.
cultural_context: Common but impolite. Origin debated: may refer to half of '500' (complete idiot) or ancient currency units.
example_usage: 你真是个二百五。(Nǐ zhēnshi ge èrbǎiwǔ.) - You're really an idiot.

meaning: Refers to annoying, gossipy, or meddlesome women. References March 8th (International Women's Day) twisted into insult.
cultural_context: Sexist term targeting women's behavior. Implies they talk too much or interfere in others' business.
example_usage: 别这么三八！(Bié zhème sānbā!) - Don't be so nosy!

meaning: Woman who appears innocent/pure but is actually calculating and promiscuous. Green tea symbolizes fake purity.
cultural_context: Internet-age insult popularized on social media. Criticizes women who use innocence as a weapon.
example_usage: 她是个绿茶婊。(Tā shì ge lǜchábiǎo.) - She's a fake innocent girl.

meaning: Young male of mediocre appearance and social standing. Often used ironically or self-deprecatingly.
cultural_context: Originally vulgar but reclaimed by internet culture. Describes average guys who aren't rich/handsome/powerful.
example_usage: 我就是个屌丝。(Wǒ jiùshì ge diǎosī.) - I'm just a loser.

meaning: Person who constantly attacks others online, 'spraying' insults everywhere.
cultural_context: Describes toxic internet users who criticize everything. Common on Weibo, Bilibili, gaming platforms.
example_usage: 别理那个喷子。(Bié lǐ nàge pēnzi.) - Don't mind that troll.

meaning: Derogatory term for Japanese people. References their shorter stature and smaller country size.
cultural_context: WWII-era slur still used today. Can provoke strong reactions. Some Japanese find it less offensive than intended.
example_usage: 小日本！(Xiǎo Rìběn!) - You Jap!

meaning: Derogatory Taiwanese term for mainland Chinese, referencing communism.
cultural_context: Used primarily in Taiwan to describe mainland Chinese negatively. Politically charged term.
example_usage: 那些阿共仔...(Nàxiē ā gòng zǎi...) - Those commies...
